fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship a coordinate utility wrapper for our maps SDK integration. Basically we keep copy-pasting the same spherical math and polyline wire-format stuff across every project and it's getting messy. The idea is a clean adapter layer that devs can drop in and call without worrying about the underlying geometry plumbing.

We need it to handle the usual suspects: encoding/decoding those compact path strings, figuring out if a shape is closed, trimming down paths to fewer points when the detail isn't needed, all the bearing/distance/offset math, interpolating between two points, area calculations (including the signed version for winding order checks), point-in-polygon queries, edge proximity checks, and the map object wrappers for lines and polygons. Oh and don't forget the heat-map weight conversion thing and the flat point component accessor — Marcus mentioned those got dropped in the last sprint.

Coordinate precision should be consistent everywhere, same as what we agreed on for the location service outputs. For the simplification feature, the tolerance is distance-based, not angle-based, just like we handled it in the route-matching module before.

The adapter needs to run as a standalone black-box process: read a JSON command from stdin, write the result to stdout. Output format rules should follow the same conventions we nailed down for the geo-search results endpoint. Please keep the core logic fully decoupled from the I/O layer so we can unit test it independently.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: anytime we decode or compute a latitude/longitude, it needs to be formatted to exactly six decimal places, everywhere, no exceptions. So think values like 1.000000 and -90.000000, and when we echo a query point back it should be in the form lat,lng with those same six decimal places. That applies across the board and should stay aligned with the feature test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the usual Google Maps API coordinate precision conventions.

A couple output specifics too. For the polyline encoding piece in Feature 1, print the encoded polyline string to stdout and make sure it has a trailing newline. For a single point like lat=1.0, lng=2.0, the output is the Google Encoded Polyline Algorithm string for that coordinate sequence, printed to stdout with a trailing newline. For path length in Feature 10, print total meters as a floating-point value with at least one decimal place, and if the coordinate list is empty or only has one coordinate, the output should be '0.0'. Same general idea for area: Feature 11 prints the unsigned area in square meters as a floating-point number with at least one decimal place, and Feature 12 prints the signed area for the given traversal on the first line and the signed area for the reversed traversal on the second line, with opposite signs.

For the polygon and edge-style checks, the text output should stay super literal. Feature 13 prints 'inside' or 'outside' on the first line, then the query coordinate as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places on the second line. Feature 14 and Feature 15 print 'on_edge' if the query point lies on or within the default tolerance of the edge/path, or 'not_on_edge' otherwise, and then the query coordinate as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places on the next line. The map wrappers should mirror that behavior: Feature 16 prints 'on_edge' or 'not_on_edge' then the query coordinate, Feature 17 prints the total length in meters with at least one decimal place and should print '0.0' for an empty line, Feature 18 prints 'inside' or 'outside' then the query coordinate, and Feature 19 prints 'on_edge' or 'not_on_edge' then the query coordinate.

Last couple details: Feature 20 should print the stroke width as a float, the stroke color as an integer, and the count of vertices, each on a separate line. Example values are '1.0', '-16777216', '1'. Feature 21 should print the x component followed by the y component, each on a separate line, formatted as floating-point numbers like '1.0' and '2.0'. And for simplification in Feature 4, the tolerance is definitely tolerance_meters and it should use a spherical-distance-based Ramer–Douglas–Peucker algorithm, not an angular or planar tolerance.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun main() — stdin JSON dispatch adapter routing to domain modules: PolylineCodec, PolygonAnalyzer, SphericalCalculator, PathSimplifier, MapObjectAdapter, HeatMapConverter

trigger_keywords: decode output format
what does decoding print
decoded coordinate count
polyline decode result
how to format decoded path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The decoding feature (Feature 2) must first print the number of decoded coordinates on its own line, then print each coordinate on a separate line in the format 'lat,lng' with six decimal places each (e.g., '1.000000,2.000000').

trigger_keywords: closed polygon output
polygon closedness format
how to report closed
open vs closed output
first and last coordinate echo
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The closed polygon detection feature (Feature 3) must print 'closed' or 'open' on the first line, then print the first coordinate and last coordinate each on their own line in 'lat,lng' format with six decimal places, regardless of whether the polygon is closed or open.

trigger_keywords: simplification output
what does simplify print
simplified path result format
tolerance units
path simplification distance
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path simplification feature (Feature 4) uses a tolerance expressed in meters (not degrees or radians). The output must print the count of retained coordinates on the first line, then print the first retained coordinate and the last retained coordinate each on their own line in 'lat,lng' format with six decimal places.

trigger_keywords: heading output
bearing format
spherical heading degrees
what range is heading
clockwise from north output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The spherical heading feature (Feature 5) must print the bearing in degrees clockwise from north, normalized to the signed degree range (i.e., values in [-180, 180]), formatted to six decimal places, followed by a newline.

trigger_keywords: offset output format
destination coordinate print
spherical offset result
what does offset print
travel distance output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The spherical offset feature (Feature 6) must print the resulting destination coordinate as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places on a single line followed by a newline.

trigger_keywords: offset origin output
recover origin format
origin available check
what does offset origin print
origin coordinate result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The spherical offset origin feature (Feature 7) must first print 'true' or 'false' indicating whether a valid origin is available. If true, it must print the recovered coordinate on the next line as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places.

trigger_keywords: interpolation output
spherical interpolate result
fraction coordinate print
what does interpolate output
slerp result format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The spherical interpolation feature (Feature 8) must print the interpolated coordinate as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places. For fraction=0.0 return the start coordinate, for fraction=1.0 return the end coordinate, for fraction=0.5 return the midpoint on the great circle.

trigger_keywords: distance output format
spherical distance meters
what does distance print
how many decimal places for distance
distance result formatting
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The spherical distance feature (Feature 9) must print the distance in meters as a floating-point number with sufficient precision (at least one decimal place) followed by a newline. The Earth radius used must match the Google Maps SDK value of approximately 6371009.0 meters.

trigger_keywords: heat map point output
weighted heat point format
intensity default value
heat point coordinate print
heat map conversion result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The weighted heat-map point feature (Feature 22) must print the coordinate as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places on the first line, and the intensity value as a float on the second line. When no intensity is provided, the default intensity must be 1.0.

trigger_keywords: stdin routing
how are features dispatched
json command structure
feature field in json
how does the adapter select the operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The JSON must contain a 'feature' field (integer 1–22) identifying which operation to perform, plus the operation-specific fields described in each feature's test case input. The adapter routes to the appropriate core domain function and writes the result to stdout.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: dispatcher input contract
feature routing
empty expected output
maps adapter stdin
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads one JSON object from stdin. Public case files are grouped by feature filename (feature1_... through feature22_...), and inputs contain the fields for that feature directly (for example points, encoded_polyline, start/end, path, polygon, line, query, coordinate, x/y, or intensity). Do not require a generic top-level 'action' field or a top-level numeric 'feature' field. If a public case has an empty expected_output string, the correct behavior for that case is to emit no stdout.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Marcus mentioned those got dropped in the last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 21 (planar point x/y component accessor) and Feature 22 (weighted heat-map point conversion from geographic coordinate) were the two items deferred; they must be included in this delivery.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the signed version for winding order checks
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 12 (signed area orientation) must compute the signed spherical polygon area for both the original traversal order and the reversed traversal order, and the two signed values must have opposite signs, demonstrating correct winding-order sensitivity.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same conventions we nailed down for the geo-search results endpoint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Output formatting rules: one result per line to stdout, no JSON wrapping in output, coordinate pairs printed as 'lat,lng' with six decimal places, boolean flags as lowercase 'true'/'false', counts as plain integers, distances and areas as plain floats.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected input/output contract, and refer to the start.md PRD for the feature-level descriptions. If the behavior is still unclear, align with the most conservative interpretation (e.g., six decimal places for coordinates, meters for distances) and flag it for review.

feature10_path_length.json.description: Compute total spherical length for an ordered coordinate path.

feature11_spherical_polygon_area.json.description: Compute the unsigned area enclosed by a spherical polygon.

feature12_signed_area_orientation.json.description: Report opposite signed areas when a spherical polygon traversal order is reversed.

feature13_polygon_containment.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate lies inside a geodesic polygon.

feature14_polygon_edge.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate lies on or near a polygon edge.

feature15_path_location.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate lies on or near a geodesic path.

feature16_map_line_containment.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate lies on a map line object.

feature17_map_line_length.json.description: Read the spherical length represented by a map line object.

feature18_map_polygon_containment.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate is contained by a map polygon object.

feature19_map_polygon_edge.json.description: Classify whether a query coordinate lies on or near a map polygon object edge.

feature1_polyline_encoding.json.description: Encode a coordinate sequence into the compact polyline wire format.

feature20_polygon_style_options.json.description: Build polygon drawing options that preserve style values and vertices.

feature21_point_components.json.description: Expose the two numeric components of a planar point in order.

feature22_weighted_heat_point.json.description: Convert a geographic coordinate into a weighted heat-map point.

feature2_polyline_decoding.json.description: Decode a compact polyline string into an ordered coordinate sequence.

feature3_polygon_closedness.json.description: Classify a coordinate sequence as closed only when the first and last coordinates are equal.

feature4_path_simplification.json.description: Simplify a coordinate path while preserving its endpoints.

feature5_spherical_heading.json.description: Compute the initial bearing between two coordinates on a sphere.

feature6_spherical_offset.json.description: Move a coordinate by a spherical distance and heading.

feature7_spherical_offset_origin.json.description: Recover a possible origin coordinate from a destination, distance, and heading.

feature8_spherical_interpolation.json.description: Interpolate along the spherical path between two coordinates.

feature9_spherical_distance.json.description: Compute spherical distance between two coordinates in meters.
